Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.
Advice Prevention: DHPP vaccination is core and highly protective. Ensure complete puppy series and triennial adult boosters. Contaminated environments should be disinfected with 1:30 bleach. Quarantine sick puppies.

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial infections; antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus involved. Nebulization, nasal decongestants, and nutritional support for recovery.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate infected cats. Clean nasal discharge regularly; use a humidifier. Reduce stress and overcrowding. Wash hands between handling sick and healthy cats.

Treatment: • the external application of antiseptics contributes to the healing of the ulcers and wards off attacks by flies. • a common and inexpensive dressing for the lesions in the feet is a mixture of coal-tar and copper sulphate in the proportion of 5:1.
Advice Prevention: • heavy milch animals and exotic breeds of cattle bred for milk should be protected regularly. • it is advisable to carry out two vaccinations at an interval of six months followed by an annual vaccination programme. • isolation and segregation of sick animals. It should be informed immediately to the veterinary doctor • disinfection of animal sheds with bleaching powder or phenol • attendants and equipment’s for sick animals should be ideally separate • the equipment’s should be thoroughly sanitized • proper disposal of left over feed by the animal • proper disposal of carcasses • control of flies

Treatment: Treatment varies according to the condition. The following are some medicines that may be used; ; Intravenous or subcutaneous fluids in extreme cases of dehydration; Antibiotics in the presence of an infection; oral steroids such as prednisolone in the event of inflammation
Advice Prevention: Severe diarrhoea or vomiting may lead to dehydration. It is important to consult your veterinarian immediately.; offer your pet lots of fresh water to maintain hydration.; Change in diet is crucial to decrease digestive tract problems. Choose food which is high in soluble and insoluble fibre to be easily digestible.; Never feed your pet scrapes of skin or other left over’s which are not good for you. If they are not good for your neither are for your pet.

Treatment: Systemic antifungals based on fungus type: itraconazole or fluconazole for dermatophytes and yeast; terbinafine for ringworm; amphotericin B for systemic fungal infections. Topical antifungal shampoos and creams as adjuncts.
Advice Prevention: Keep skin dry and clean; treat all in-contact animals for ringworm simultaneously. Wear gloves during treatment. Disinfect environment with dilute bleach. Boost immune health through proper nutrition.

Treatment: Doxycycline (10 mg/kg orally once daily for 28-30 days) is the treatment of choice. Amoxicillin or azithromycin as alternatives. NSAIDs for joint pain relief. Repeat C6 antibody test at 6 months to assess response.
Advice Prevention: Lyme disease vaccination for dogs in endemic areas. Monthly tick preventives (isoxazolines: afoxolaner, fluralaner, sarolaner). Daily tick checks and prompt removal. Avoid wooded/tall-grass areas during peak tick season.

Treatment: Specific dewormers based on parasite: fenbendazole or pyrantel pamoate for roundworms/hookworms; praziquantel for tapeworms; metronidazole or fenbendazole for Giardia. Repeat treatment after 2-3 weeks.
Advice Prevention: Routine fecal examination twice yearly; monthly broad-spectrum parasite preventives. Clean litter box daily; prevent hunting of prey animals which carry tapeworm larvae.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Treatment: Intramammary antibiotic infusion (cloxacillin, penicillin-streptomycin, or cefapirin) directly into the affected teat after complete milk-out; systemic antibiotics (penicillin, amoxicillin) for severe cases with fever. NSAIDs (flunixin meglumine) for pain and inflammation.
Advice Prevention: Implement a dry-cow therapy program; dip teats in germicidal post-milking iodine solution after every milking. Maintain clean, dry bedding; cull chronically infected cows. Regular somatic cell count monitoring of bulk milk.

Treatment: Intensive supportive care: IV fluids to correct dehydration, broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent sepsis, antiemetics (maropitant), and tube feeding if anorexic. Plasma or blood transfusions for severely immunosuppressed cats.
Advice Prevention: FVRCP vaccination is core and highly protective. Isolate sick cats immediately. Decontaminate exposed areas with 1:32 bleach dilution as the virus is hardy in the environment.

Treatment: Doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 10 days) for Bordetella; cough suppressants (hydrocodone, butorphanol) for comfort. Severe or complicated cases (pneumonia) may need hospitalization and IV antibiotics.
Advice Prevention: Bordetella, parainfluenza, and adenovirus-2 vaccines required before kennel stays or group activities. Ensure good kennel ventilation. Disinfect shared items. Isolate coughing dogs.

Treatment: Intensive supportive care: IV fluids to correct dehydration, broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent sepsis, antiemetics (maropitant), and tube feeding if anorexic. Plasma or blood transfusions for severely immunosuppressed cats.
Advice Prevention: FVRCP vaccination is core and highly protective. Isolate sick cats immediately. Decontaminate exposed areas with 1:32 bleach dilution as the virus is hardy in the environment.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Intensive IV fluid and electrolyte replacement to correct dehydration and hypovolemia. Antiemetics (maropitant 1 mg/kg SC daily); antibiotics (ampicillin + enrofloxacin) to prevent sepsis. Plasma or blood transfusion for hypoproteinemia.
Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: • Treatment is not necessary; it commonly heals in about 8 weeks. • Imidazole spray may stop progression of lesions and slow the spread to other cattle.
Advice Prevention: • Management — prevent mixing cattle with active ringworm infection and isolate any cases. • Management — process affected animals last in handling facilities and treat with imidazole spray at each yarding.

Treatment: Doxycycline (10 mg/kg orally once daily for 14-28 days) is effective for Ehrlichia, Anaplasma, and Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever. Supportive care with fluids and blood transfusion for thrombocytopenia or anemia.
Advice Prevention: Year-round tick prevention with isoxazolines (NexGard, Bravecto, Simparica). Daily tick checks after outdoor activities; remove ticks with fine-tipped tweezers. Avoid wooded areas during peak tick season (spring/fall).

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial infections; antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus involved. Nebulization, nasal decongestants, and nutritional support for recovery.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate infected cats. Clean nasal discharge regularly; use a humidifier. Reduce stress and overcrowding. Wash hands between handling sick and healthy cats.

Advice Prevention: FVRCP vaccination is core and highly protective. Isolate sick cats immediately. Decontaminate exposed areas with 1:32 bleach dilution as the virus is hardy in the environment.

Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Non-steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Agents; carprofen, robenacoxib; Steroids; prednisolone Arthritis is not curable however preventing further erosion is paramount, since it limits the chance of needing surgery in the future.
Advice Prevention: It is important to stipulate to the correct dosing and not try to adjust it to provide more pain relief.; Be careful when purchasing any over-the-counter medication.; Supplement your pet’s diet with omega 3 and 6 fatty acids, glucosamine and chondroitin; Large breed dogs* are more prone to arthritis, thus it is important to maintain a healthy diet from the start. Provide specifically designed food so that growth takes place at an appropriate rate, since if it occurs too quickly joints might form in an abnormal manner eventually leading to joint disease.; Certain cat breeds are also more prone to Degenerative Joint Disease, such as Himalayan, Siamese and Persian.; Arthritis can be prevented by maintaining your pet with a healthy weight. *Golden retrievers, Rottweilers, Labrador retrievers, German shepherds

Treatment: Famciclovir (antiviral) orally for active outbreaks; topical idoxuridine or trifluridine eye drops for ocular herpes. L-lysine supplementation (500 mg/day) may reduce viral replication. Antibiotics for secondary infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccine reduces severity of disease. Minimize stress (a key reactivation trigger). Use lysine-supplemented food as long-term management.

Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.
Advice Prevention: DHPP vaccination is core and highly protective. Ensure complete puppy series and triennial adult boosters. Contaminated environments should be disinfected with 1:30 bleach. Quarantine sick puppies.

Treatment: No cure; management includes chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, prednisolone) for FeLV-associated lymphoma. Supportive care: blood transfusions for anemia, antibiotics for infections, appetite stimulants.
Advice Prevention: FeLV vaccination is strongly recommended for outdoor or at-risk cats. Test all new cats before household introduction; isolate positive cats from FeLV-negative animals.

Treatment: Intramammary antibiotic infusion (cloxacillin, penicillin-streptomycin, or cefapirin) directly into the affected teat after complete milk-out; systemic antibiotics (penicillin, amoxicillin) for severe cases with fever. NSAIDs (flunixin meglumine) for pain and inflammation.
Advice Prevention: Implement a dry-cow therapy program; dip teats in germicidal post-milking iodine solution after every milking. Maintain clean, dry bedding; cull chronically infected cows. Regular somatic cell count monitoring of bulk milk.

Treatment: Melarsomine injections (split-dose protocol) to kill adult worms; doxycycline pretreatment. Strict exercise restriction for 2 months post-treatment. Corticosteroids if pulmonary reaction occurs.
Advice Prevention: Year-round monthly heartworm prevention is the most effective strategy. Annual heartworm antigen testing before refilling prevention. Limit mosquito exposure.

Treatment: GS-441524 (antiviral, 4-6 mg/kg SQ or orally) for 12 weeks is the current gold-standard treatment with high remission rates. Prednisolone as supportive anti-inflammatory; drainage of effusions for comfort.
Advice Prevention: Reduce fecal-oral transmission of FCoV in multi-cat households (daily litter box cleaning). Minimize stress. An intranasal FIP vaccine is available in some countries but efficacy is debated.

Treatment: No curative treatment; supportive management with quality nutrition, probiotics, and reduced stress. Antidiarrheal agents may provide temporary relief. Culling of clinical cases is recommended.
Advice Prevention: Annual herd testing with test-and-cull for positive animals. Feed calves from low-risk cows; ensure clean colostrum. Prevent manure contamination of feed and water sources.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Treatment: Topical antibiotic eye drops (tobramycin or chloramphenicol) for bacterial cases; antiviral drops (idoxuridine) if herpesvirus is involved. Warm saline cleaning of discharge and lubrication eye drops to relieve irritation.
Advice Prevention: Keep eyes clean daily; isolate from other cats during active infection. Vaccinate against herpesvirus (FVRCP) as it is a leading cause. Minimize stress which can reactivate latent herpesvirus.

Treatment: Fluid and electrolyte replacement (oral or IV) is the cornerstone of treatment. Antibiotics (trimethoprim-sulfonamide, florfenicol, or ampicillin) based on sensitivity testing. NSAIDs to control fever.
Advice Prevention: Biosecurity: quarantine new animals, rodent control, clean feed storage. Vaccination available for some serovars (S. Dublin, S. Typhimurium). Proper manure management and hygiene in calving areas.

Treatment: Corticosteroids (prednisolone) for airway inflammation; bronchodilators (theophylline, terbutaline) to relieve bronchoconstriction. Antitussives for comfort. Weight loss therapy if obese.
Advice Prevention: Eliminate exposure to cigarette smoke, aerosols, and dusty environments. Use a harness instead of a neck collar to reduce airway pressure. Keep dog at healthy body weight. Annual chest X-rays to monitor progression.

Treatment: Doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate for bacterial infections; nasal decongestants and saline flushes. Antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus suspected. Syringe feeding and appetite stimulants if not eating.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate sick cats promptly. Provide warm, draft-free rest area. Regular cleaning of nasal discharge to keep airways clear.

Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.
Advice Prevention: DHPP vaccination is core and highly protective. Ensure complete puppy series and triennial adult boosters. Contaminated environments should be disinfected with 1:30 bleach. Quarantine sick puppies.

Treatment: • Management — other short-term infected cattle will usually recover, they might need to be monitored for respiratory diseases like pneumonia. • Humane destruction — PI and MD calves should be humanely euthanized
Advice Prevention: • Vaccinations — vaccines are available, but more widely used in breeding herd before joining. • Management — avoid mixing susceptible cattle, especially when their BVDV status is unknown. • Management — introducing PI cattle to healthy, non-pregnant cows is sometimes used to immunize the herd, but individual animal’s exposure is unknown.

Advice Prevention: Bordetella, parainfluenza, and adenovirus-2 vaccines required before kennel stays or group activities. Ensure good kennel ventilation. Disinfect shared items. Isolate coughing dogs.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV/SQ fluid therapy, nutritional support, and appetite stimulants. NSAIDs (meloxicam) for fever and oral ulcer pain; antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination is the most effective preventive. Isolate infected cats immediately and disinfect shared surfaces with bleach solution. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat households.

Treatment: Primarily supportive: aggressive oral or IV electrolyte/fluid replacement is critical. Halofuginone (100 mcg/kg orally) given daily for the first 7 days of life reduces oocyst shedding. No fully curative drug available.
Advice Prevention: Ensure adequate colostrum intake within the first 6 hours (provides passive immunity). Isolate diarrheic calves; disinfect calving pens thoroughly. Wash hands after handling affected calves.

Treatment: Doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 10-14 days) or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Cough suppressants (butorphanol) for comfort; nebulization with saline for severe cases. Rest from exercise during recovery.
Advice Prevention: Bordetella vaccination (intranasal or injectable) before kenneling, grooming, or dog-park exposure. Avoid contact with coughing dogs. Good ventilation in kennels.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment exists. Remove BLV-positive animals from herd to prevent spread. Supportive care for secondary complications such as lymphoma.
Advice Prevention: Test all animals before introduction to the herd. Use single-use needles; disinfect equipment between animals. Control blood-sucking insects. Avoid sharing needles, rectal sleeves, or dehorning equipment.

Treatment: Antihistamines (cetirizine 5-10 mg daily, diphenhydramine) for mild cases; corticosteroids (prednisolone) for moderate-severe flares. Nasal saline flushes; allergen-specific immunotherapy (desensitization injections) for long-term control.
Advice Prevention: Identify and minimize allergen exposure: pollen, mold, dust mites. Use HEPA air purifiers indoors. Wipe paws after outdoor walks. Regular bathing to remove surface allergens.

Treatment: • Veterinary care — involving administration of sulphadimidine through injection or drench.
Advice Prevention: • Diet — provide feed and water off the ground to minimize contamination. • Diet — include sulphadimidine or ionophore like monensin, lasolocid in feeds. • Management and environment — rotation of paddock between species may reduce build-up of oocytes. oral drinking, 5g/10kg weight - Hancoli-Forte, oral drinking, 2g/10kg weight - Gentacostrim, 2g/10kg weight Besides, we can use antibiotics for preventing intestinal bacteria; or herbs such as tea bud, guava leaf to recover the intestinal membrane.

Treatment: Fluid therapy (IV or SQ at home) to support kidney function; phosphate binders (aluminum hydroxide, lanthanum carbonate); ACE inhibitors (benazepril) to reduce proteinuria. Prescription renal diet (low phosphorus, controlled protein).
Advice Prevention: Encourage water intake with water fountains or wet food. Annual blood and urine panels for early detection. Avoid nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides). Schedule biannual vet checks for cats over 7 years.

Treatment: Supportive care: anti-inflammatories (flunixin meglumine), bronchodilators, and oxygen therapy for severe cases. Antibiotics for secondary bacterial pneumonia.
Advice Prevention: BRSV vaccination in cow-calf programs (vaccinate dams pre-calving for passive immunity). Avoid overcrowding calves. Ensure colostrum intake within 6 hours of birth.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Treatment: Famciclovir (30-40 mg/kg orally twice daily) for 3 weeks; topical idoxuridine drops for eye involvement. Supportive care includes fluid therapy, nutritional support, and environmental humidification.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP booster vaccination. L-lysine as a dietary supplement. Identify and reduce stress triggers (moving, new pets, boarding) that reactivate latent infection.

Treatment: Doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 10 days) for Bordetella; cough suppressants (hydrocodone, butorphanol) for comfort. Severe or complicated cases (pneumonia) may need hospitalization and IV antibiotics.

Treatment: Early antibiotic therapy is essential: tulathromycin (2.5 mg/kg SC, single dose), florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on sensitivity. NSAIDs (meloxicam or flunixin meglumine) for anti-inflammation and fever.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate against major respiratory pathogens (BRSV, BPI3, IBR, BVD, Mannheimia). Ensure proper barn ventilation and avoid overcrowding. Minimize weaning and transport stress.

Advice Prevention: FVRCP vaccination (core) is highly effective; start series at 6-8 weeks. Isolate unvaccinated kittens from all feline contact. Use parvocide disinfectants (dilute bleach) in contaminated areas.

Treatment: Supportive care: rest, fluids, and nutritional support. Antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial pneumonia; antivirals in severe cases. NSAIDs for fever management.
Advice Prevention: Bivalent canine influenza vaccine (H3N2 and H3N8 strains). Isolate sick dogs immediately; disinfect shared items. Notify boarding facilities or dog-park contacts of exposure.

Treatment: Anti-inflammatory therapy (NSAIDs), supportive fluids, and antibiotics (tulathromycin, florfenicol) for secondary bacterial infections. Severe cases may need bronchodilators.
Advice Prevention: Include BRSV in vaccination protocols. Reduce herd stress; ensure adequate ventilation. Isolate newly purchased animals for at least 3 weeks before mixing with herd.

Treatment: Infected cattle should be culled under national TB eradication programs; no routine antibiotic treatment is recommended as it masks infection and contributes to persistence. Notify veterinary authorities upon suspected diagnosis.
Advice Prevention: Annual tuberculin testing and prompt removal of reactors. Purchase only from accredited TB-free herds. Minimize contact with wildlife. Ensure good biosecurity and ventilation in housing. Do not use unpasteurized milk from untested cows.

Treatment: Treatment varies according to the condition. The following are some medicines that may be used; ; Intravenous or subcutaneous fluids in extreme cases of dehydration; Antibiotics in the presence of an infection; oral steroids such as prednisolone in the event of inflammation
Advice Prevention: Severe diarrhoea or vomiting may lead to dehydration. It is important to consult your veterinarian immediately.; offer your pet lots of fresh water to maintain hydration.; Change in diet is crucial to decrease digestive tract problems. Choose food which is high in soluble and insoluble fibre to be easily digestible.; Never feed your pet scrapes of skin or other left over’s which are not good for you. If they are not good for your neither are for your pet.

Treatment: Methimazole (2.5 mg orally twice daily) as first-line medical management; transdermal gel formulation available for difficult cats. Radioactive iodine (I-131) is the curative gold standard. Surgical thyroidectomy is an alternative.
Advice Prevention: Monitor thyroid levels every 3-6 months after starting treatment. Watch for weight loss and increased appetite as early signs. Diet low in iodine (Hills y/d) can be used as adjunct therapy.

Treatment: • Veterinary care — involving administration of sulphadimidine through injection or drench.
Advice Prevention: • Diet — provide feed and water off the ground to minimize contamination. • Diet — include sulphadimidine or ionophore like monensin, lasolocid in feeds. • Management and environment — rotation of paddock between species may reduce build-up of oocytes. oral drinking, 5g/10kg weight - Hancoli-Forte, oral drinking, 2g/10kg weight - Gentacostrim, 2g/10kg weight Besides, we can use antibiotics for preventing intestinal bacteria; or herbs such as tea bud, guava leaf to recover the intestinal membrane.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV fluids with dextrose, hepatoprotectants (SAMe, milk thistle/silymarin), broad-spectrum antibiotics. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs. Plasma transfusion for coagulopathy.
Advice Prevention: Core CAV-2 vaccine provides cross-protection against CAV-1 (hepatitis virus). Annual booster as part of DHPP vaccination. Isolate infected dogs.

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial infections; antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus involved. Nebulization, nasal decongestants, and nutritional support for recovery.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate infected cats. Clean nasal discharge regularly; use a humidifier. Reduce stress and overcrowding. Wash hands between handling sick and healthy cats.

Treatment: NSAIDs (flunixin meglumine 2.2 mg/kg IV) for fever reduction and pain; supportive fluid therapy. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (tulathromycin, florfenicol) to prevent secondary bacterial pneumonia.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate before high-risk periods (transport, mixing groups). Minimize stress and overcrowding during housing. Ensure adequate ventilation in barns. Quarantine newly purchased animals for 3 weeks.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV/SQ fluid therapy, nutritional support, and appetite stimulants. NSAIDs (meloxicam) for fever and oral ulcer pain; antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination is the most effective preventive. Isolate infected cats immediately and disinfect shared surfaces with bleach solution. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat households.

Treatment: Treatment varies according to the condition. The following are some medicines that may be used; ; Intravenous or subcutaneous fluids in extreme cases of dehydration; Antibiotics in the presence of an infection; oral steroids such as prednisolone in the event of inflammation

Treatment: Doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate for bacterial infections; nasal decongestants and saline flushes. Antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus suspected. Syringe feeding and appetite stimulants if not eating.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate sick cats promptly. Provide warm, draft-free rest area. Regular cleaning of nasal discharge to keep airways clear.

Treatment: No curative treatment; supportive management with quality nutrition, probiotics, and reduced stress. Antidiarrheal agents may provide temporary relief. Culling of clinical cases is recommended.
Advice Prevention: Annual herd testing with test-and-cull for positive animals. Feed calves from low-risk cows; ensure clean colostrum. Prevent manure contamination of feed and water sources.

Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.
Advice Prevention: DHPP vaccination is core and highly protective. Ensure complete puppy series and triennial adult boosters. Contaminated environments should be disinfected with 1:30 bleach. Quarantine sick puppies.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV/SQ fluid therapy, nutritional support, and appetite stimulants. NSAIDs (meloxicam) for fever and oral ulcer pain; antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination is the most effective preventive. Isolate infected cats immediately and disinfect shared surfaces with bleach solution. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat households.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Treatment varies according to the condition. The following are some medicines that may be used; ; Intravenous or subcutaneous fluids in extreme cases of dehydration; Antibiotics in the presence of an infection; oral steroids such as prednisolone in the event of inflammation

Treatment: Famciclovir (30-40 mg/kg orally twice daily) for 3 weeks; topical idoxuridine drops for eye involvement. Supportive care includes fluid therapy, nutritional support, and environmental humidification.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP booster vaccination. L-lysine as a dietary supplement. Identify and reduce stress triggers (moving, new pets, boarding) that reactivate latent infection.

Treatment: No effective curative treatment available. Supportive nutrition with high-energy, easily digestible feed can extend productive life. Cull confirmed-positive animals to prevent herd spread.
Advice Prevention: Implement a Johne's disease herd management program: annual ELISA testing, test-and-cull strategy. Prevent fecal contamination of feed and water. Separate calves from adult cows at birth.

Treatment: Intensive IV fluid and electrolyte replacement to correct dehydration and hypovolemia. Antiemetics (maropitant 1 mg/kg SC daily); antibiotics (ampicillin + enrofloxacin) to prevent sepsis. Plasma or blood transfusion for hypoproteinemia.
Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline, azithromycin) targeting Chlamydophila and Bordetella; antiviral famciclovir for herpesvirus. Nebulization therapy with saline helps loosen secretions. Appetite stimulants if anorexic.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP (covers FHV-1 and FCV). Isolate affected cats; disinfect bowls and bedding. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat environments.

Treatment: There is no practical curative treatment for bovine tuberculosis. Infected cattle must be slaughtered under official disease eradication programs. Antibiotics (isoniazid, streptomycin) are used only in exceptional circumstances under strict veterinary supervision.
Advice Prevention: Implement a test-and-slaughter program using the tuberculin skin test (TST). Purchase animals only from TB-free herds. Prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs (badgers, deer). Pasteurize all milk. Regular herd testing as per national regulations.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV fluids with dextrose, hepatoprotectants (SAMe, milk thistle/silymarin), broad-spectrum antibiotics. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs. Plasma transfusion for coagulopathy.
Advice Prevention: Core CAV-2 vaccine provides cross-protection against CAV-1 (hepatitis virus). Annual booster as part of DHPP vaccination. Isolate infected dogs.

Treatment: Famciclovir (antiviral) is the preferred oral treatment; topical antiviral eye drops for corneal ulcers. L-lysine supplementation; broad-spectrum antibiotics for secondary bacterial sinusitis.
Advice Prevention: Core FVRCP vaccination reduces clinical severity. Long-term L-lysine supplementation may reduce viral recurrence. Stress is the primary trigger for reactivation — manage environmental stressors.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 10 days) for Bordetella; cough suppressants (hydrocodone, butorphanol) for comfort. Severe or complicated cases (pneumonia) may need hospitalization and IV antibiotics.
Advice Prevention: Bordetella, parainfluenza, and adenovirus-2 vaccines required before kennel stays or group activities. Ensure good kennel ventilation. Disinfect shared items. Isolate coughing dogs.

Treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial component; supportive care with humidification and nasal decongestants. Nutritional support with syringe feeding if anorexic.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination; good ventilation in cat housing. Disinfect shared items between cats. Keep new cats isolated for 2 weeks before introduction.

Treatment: There is no practical curative treatment for bovine tuberculosis. Infected cattle must be slaughtered under official disease eradication programs. Antibiotics (isoniazid, streptomycin) are used only in exceptional circumstances under strict veterinary supervision.
Advice Prevention: Implement a test-and-slaughter program using the tuberculin skin test (TST). Purchase animals only from TB-free herds. Prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs (badgers, deer). Pasteurize all milk. Regular herd testing as per national regulations.

Treatment: Intensive IV fluid and electrolyte replacement to correct dehydration and hypovolemia. Antiemetics (maropitant 1 mg/kg SC daily); antibiotics (ampicillin + enrofloxacin) to prevent sepsis. Plasma or blood transfusion for hypoproteinemia.
Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: GS-441524 (antiviral, 4-6 mg/kg SQ or orally) for 12 weeks is the current gold-standard treatment with high remission rates. Prednisolone as supportive anti-inflammatory; drainage of effusions for comfort.
Advice Prevention: Reduce fecal-oral transmission of FCoV in multi-cat households (daily litter box cleaning). Minimize stress. An intranasal FIP vaccine is available in some countries but efficacy is debated.

Treatment: No effective curative treatment available. Supportive nutrition with high-energy, easily digestible feed can extend productive life. Cull confirmed-positive animals to prevent herd spread.
Advice Prevention: Implement a Johne's disease herd management program: annual ELISA testing, test-and-cull strategy. Prevent fecal contamination of feed and water. Separate calves from adult cows at birth.

Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV/SQ fluid therapy, nutritional support, and appetite stimulants. NSAIDs (meloxicam) for fever and oral ulcer pain; antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination is the most effective preventive. Isolate infected cats immediately and disinfect shared surfaces with bleach solution. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat households.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Treatment: Immunostimulants (Lymphocyte T-Cell Immunomodulator, LTCI) and supportive care. Antiviral treatments (zidovudine) in some cases. Treat all secondary infections aggressively with antibiotics or antifungals.
Advice Prevention: FeLV vaccination is the primary preventive. Quarantine and test any new cat entering the home. Spay/neuter to reduce fighting and virus transmission.

Treatment: There is no practical curative treatment for bovine tuberculosis. Infected cattle must be slaughtered under official disease eradication programs. Antibiotics (isoniazid, streptomycin) are used only in exceptional circumstances under strict veterinary supervision.
Advice Prevention: Implement a test-and-slaughter program using the tuberculin skin test (TST). Purchase animals only from TB-free herds. Prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs (badgers, deer). Pasteurize all milk. Regular herd testing as per national regulations.

Treatment: Penicillin G or amoxicillin for the acute bacteremic phase; doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 2 weeks) to clear renal shedding. Aggressive IV fluid therapy for kidney/liver support. Monitor electrolytes closely.
Advice Prevention: Annual leptospirosis vaccination. Avoid walking dogs near rivers, flooded areas, or wildlife habitats. Rodent control. Wear gloves when handling urine from infected dogs.

Treatment: Famciclovir (antiviral) orally for active outbreaks; topical idoxuridine or trifluridine eye drops for ocular herpes. L-lysine supplementation (500 mg/day) may reduce viral replication. Antibiotics for secondary infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccine reduces severity of disease. Minimize stress (a key reactivation trigger). Use lysine-supplemented food as long-term management.

Treatment: No effective curative treatment available. Supportive nutrition with high-energy, easily digestible feed can extend productive life. Cull confirmed-positive animals to prevent herd spread.
Advice Prevention: Implement a Johne's disease herd management program: annual ELISA testing, test-and-cull strategy. Prevent fecal contamination of feed and water. Separate calves from adult cows at birth.

Treatment: Ampicillin or amoxicillin (initial 2-week course) to clear leptospiremia; followed by doxycycline (4 weeks) to eliminate renal carrier state. IV fluids for renal and hepatic support; dialysis in severe renal failure.
Advice Prevention: Leptospirosis (4-serovar) vaccine annually, especially for dogs with outdoor/water exposure. Avoid contact with stagnant water and wildlife urine. Rodent control around property.

Treatment: Doxycycline (10 mg/kg once daily) for a minimum of 4-6 weeks is the treatment of choice. Topical tetracycline or chloramphenicol eye ointment applied twice daily for conjunctival involvement.
Advice Prevention: Treat all cats in the household simultaneously to prevent re-infection. A Chlamydophila felis vaccine is available; hygiene and reducing cat-to-cat contact are essential preventive measures.

Treatment: • Management — other short-term infected cattle will usually recover, they might need to be monitored for respiratory diseases like pneumonia. • Humane destruction — PI and MD calves should be humanely euthanized
Advice Prevention: • Vaccinations — vaccines are available, but more widely used in breeding herd before joining. • Management — avoid mixing susceptible cattle, especially when their BVDV status is unknown. • Management — introducing PI cattle to healthy, non-pregnant cows is sometimes used to immunize the herd, but individual animal’s exposure is unknown.

Advice Prevention: Reduce fecal-oral transmission of FCoV in multi-cat households (daily litter box cleaning). Minimize stress. An intranasal FIP vaccine is available in some countries but efficacy is debated.

Treatment: Supportive care: anti-inflammatories (flunixin meglumine), bronchodilators, and oxygen therapy for severe cases. Antibiotics for secondary bacterial pneumonia.
Advice Prevention: BRSV vaccination in cow-calf programs (vaccinate dams pre-calving for passive immunity). Avoid overcrowding calves. Ensure colostrum intake within 6 hours of birth.

Advice Prevention: Bivalent canine influenza vaccine (covers H3N2 and H3N8). Keep up-to-date vaccination; especially important for dogs that visit kennels, dog parks, or groomers.

Advice Prevention: Include BPI3 in bovine respiratory vaccination programs. Reduce stress at weaning, transport, and mixing. Good housing ventilation is critical. Process new calves promptly on arrival.

Advice Prevention: Remove airborne triggers: cigarette smoke, dusty litter, aerosols, and scented candles. Maintain healthy body weight. Annual radiographs to monitor airway changes.

Treatment: Corticosteroids (prednisolone, dexamethasone) to reduce airway inflammation; bronchodilators (albuterol, terbutaline) for acute relief. Inhaled medications via cat-specific spacers (AeroKat) are preferred for long-term management.
Advice Prevention: Remove airborne triggers: cigarette smoke, dusty litter, aerosols, and scented candles. Maintain healthy body weight. Annual radiographs to monitor airway changes.

Treatment: There is no practical curative treatment for bovine tuberculosis. Infected cattle must be slaughtered under official disease eradication programs. Antibiotics (isoniazid, streptomycin) are used only in exceptional circumstances under strict veterinary supervision.
Advice Prevention: Implement a test-and-slaughter program using the tuberculin skin test (TST). Purchase animals only from TB-free herds. Prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs (badgers, deer). Pasteurize all milk. Regular herd testing as per national regulations.

Treatment: Ampicillin or amoxicillin (initial 2-week course) to clear leptospiremia; followed by doxycycline (4 weeks) to eliminate renal carrier state. IV fluids for renal and hepatic support; dialysis in severe renal failure.
Advice Prevention: Leptospirosis (4-serovar) vaccine annually, especially for dogs with outdoor/water exposure. Avoid contact with stagnant water and wildlife urine. Rodent control around property.

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline, azithromycin) targeting Chlamydophila and Bordetella; antiviral famciclovir for herpesvirus. Nebulization therapy with saline helps loosen secretions. Appetite stimulants if anorexic.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP (covers FHV-1 and FCV). Isolate affected cats; disinfect bowls and bedding. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat environments.

Treatment: There is no practical curative treatment for bovine tuberculosis. Infected cattle must be slaughtered under official disease eradication programs. Antibiotics (isoniazid, streptomycin) are used only in exceptional circumstances under strict veterinary supervision.
Advice Prevention: Implement a test-and-slaughter program using the tuberculin skin test (TST). Purchase animals only from TB-free herds. Prevent contact with wildlife reservoirs (badgers, deer). Pasteurize all milk. Regular herd testing as per national regulations.

Treatment: Pre-treatment with doxycycline (4 weeks) to weaken Wolbachia bacteria in worms. Melarsomine dihydrochloride (adulticidal, 2-3 injections). Strict exercise restriction for 6-8 weeks post-treatment; ivermectin/milbemycin for microfilariae.
Advice Prevention: Monthly heartworm preventive year-round (ivermectin, milbemycin, selamectin, or moxidectin). Annual blood testing. Mosquito control (the vector); keep dogs indoors during peak mosquito hours.

Treatment: No cure; management includes chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, prednisolone) for FeLV-associated lymphoma. Supportive care: blood transfusions for anemia, antibiotics for infections, appetite stimulants.
Advice Prevention: FeLV vaccination is strongly recommended for outdoor or at-risk cats. Test all new cats before household introduction; isolate positive cats from FeLV-negative animals.

Treatment: Doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 10 days) for Bordetella; cough suppressants (hydrocodone, butorphanol) for comfort. Severe or complicated cases (pneumonia) may need hospitalization and IV antibiotics.
Advice Prevention: Bordetella, parainfluenza, and adenovirus-2 vaccines required before kennel stays or group activities. Ensure good kennel ventilation. Disinfect shared items. Isolate coughing dogs.

Treatment: GS-441524 (antiviral, 4-6 mg/kg SQ or orally) for 12 weeks is the current gold-standard treatment with high remission rates. Prednisolone as supportive anti-inflammatory; drainage of effusions for comfort.
Advice Prevention: Reduce fecal-oral transmission of FCoV in multi-cat households (daily litter box cleaning). Minimize stress. An intranasal FIP vaccine is available in some countries but efficacy is debated.

Treatment: Combination antibiotic therapy based on herd pathogen profile; NSAIDs for fever and inflammation. Monitor and re-treat non-responders within 48-72 hours with an alternative antibiotic.
Advice Prevention: Comprehensive vaccination protocol including IBR, BVD, BPI3, BRSV. Good colostrum management in calves. Processing protocols upon feedlot entry reduce BRD risk significantly.

Treatment: Intensive IV fluid and electrolyte replacement to correct dehydration and hypovolemia. Antiemetics (maropitant 1 mg/kg SC daily); antibiotics (ampicillin + enrofloxacin) to prevent sepsis. Plasma or blood transfusion for hypoproteinemia.
Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: Intensive supportive care: IV fluid and electrolyte replacement, antiemetics (maropitant), broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + enrofloxacin), and nutritional support. Whole blood or plasma transfusion for severe cases.
Advice Prevention: FVRCP vaccination (core) is highly effective; start series at 6-8 weeks. Isolate unvaccinated kittens from all feline contact. Use parvocide disinfectants (dilute bleach) in contaminated areas.

Treatment: No curative treatment; supportive management with quality nutrition, probiotics, and reduced stress. Antidiarrheal agents may provide temporary relief. Culling of clinical cases is recommended.
Advice Prevention: Annual herd testing with test-and-cull for positive animals. Feed calves from low-risk cows; ensure clean colostrum. Prevent manure contamination of feed and water sources.

Treatment: Supportive care is the only treatment option; IV fluids, anticonvulsants for seizures, antibiotics for pneumonia, and physiotherapy for neurological deficits. Ribavirin has been used experimentally.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is the most effective prevention. Avoid exposure of unvaccinated dogs to wildlife. Isolate affected dogs to prevent spread.

Treatment: Immunostimulants (Lymphocyte T-Cell Immunomodulator, LTCI) and supportive care. Antiviral treatments (zidovudine) in some cases. Treat all secondary infections aggressively with antibiotics or antifungals.
Advice Prevention: FeLV vaccination is the primary preventive. Quarantine and test any new cat entering the home. Spay/neuter to reduce fighting and virus transmission.

Treatment: No effective curative treatment available. Supportive nutrition with high-energy, easily digestible feed can extend productive life. Cull confirmed-positive animals to prevent herd spread.
Advice Prevention: Implement a Johne's disease herd management program: annual ELISA testing, test-and-cull strategy. Prevent fecal contamination of feed and water. Separate calves from adult cows at birth.

Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.
Advice Prevention: DHPP vaccination is core and highly protective. Ensure complete puppy series and triennial adult boosters. Contaminated environments should be disinfected with 1:30 bleach. Quarantine sick puppies.

Treatment: Doxycycline (10 mg/kg orally once daily) for 4-6 weeks; topical tetracycline eye ointment for ocular signs. Azithromycin is an alternative for cats that cannot tolerate doxycycline.
Advice Prevention: Treat all in-contact cats concurrently. Disinfect bedding and food bowls regularly. Consider vaccination in high-risk multi-cat environments.

Advice Prevention: FeLV vaccination is the primary preventive. Quarantine and test any new cat entering the home. Spay/neuter to reduce fighting and virus transmission.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Advice Prevention: Core Bordetella, parainfluenza, and adenovirus-2 vaccines before any dog-group activities. Disinfect shared water bowls and toys. Isolate coughing dogs from healthy ones.

Treatment: Penicillin G or amoxicillin for the acute bacteremic phase; doxycycline (5 mg/kg orally twice daily for 2 weeks) to clear renal shedding. Aggressive IV fluid therapy for kidney/liver support. Monitor electrolytes closely.
Advice Prevention: Annual leptospirosis vaccination. Avoid walking dogs near rivers, flooded areas, or wildlife habitats. Rodent control. Wear gloves when handling urine from infected dogs.

Treatment: No curative treatment; management focuses on supportive care and treating opportunistic infections promptly. Antiviral drugs (zidovudine) may slow progression. Regular blood counts guide treatment decisions.
Advice Prevention: Keep FIV-positive cats strictly indoors to prevent transmission (bite wounds) and secondary infections. Vaccinate against other pathogens. Biannual veterinary checks including blood work.

Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Famciclovir (antiviral) orally for active outbreaks; topical idoxuridine or trifluridine eye drops for ocular herpes. L-lysine supplementation (500 mg/day) may reduce viral replication. Antibiotics for secondary infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccine reduces severity of disease. Minimize stress (a key reactivation trigger). Use lysine-supplemented food as long-term management.

Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Advice Prevention: Remove airborne triggers: cigarette smoke, dusty litter, aerosols, and scented candles. Maintain healthy body weight. Annual radiographs to monitor airway changes.

Treatment: Depending on type of bacteria, the cause we select suitable medicine: - Cloxacilin 200g + Ampicillin 75mg, pump into the teat once per day, in 3 -5 days continuously - Penicillin 100.000UI + Streptomycin 1g, pump into the teat once per day, in 3 -5 days continuously - Mastijet Fort, Cloxaman, pump into the teat, 1 tube/teat, once per day, in 3 -5 days continuously - Hanocilin: Muscle injection or under skin injection 1ml/10kg weight, in 3 -5 days continuously - Hancoli: Muscle injection 1ml/10kg weight, in 3 -5 days continuously - Tetramycin *LA: Muscle injection 1ml/10kg weight, in 3 -5 days, continuously Notice: Before pumping antibiotics, we have to remove all milk out. For dry cow, beside above drugs, we can use following medicine: - Penicillin 100.000UI + Kanamycin 1g: pump into the teat once per day, in 3 -5 days continuously - Procacium penicillin 10.000UI + Furaltadone 500mg: pump into the teat once per day, in 3 -5 days continuously - Beside antibiotics we can use vitamins and other tonic medicines and keep the cow in clean place. Especially, if the cause is fungi or Mycoplasma, antibiotics will not be effective. For treatment of these cases, have to keep the cattle in hygiene environment and do milking 5 -6 times a day continuously until it recovers.
Advice Prevention: Teat dipping with iodine post-milking, dry-cow antibiotic therapy, and somatic cell count monitoring. Maintain hygienic milking equipment and clean, dry housing. Cull cows with chronic or recurrent mastitis.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: Tulathromycin, florfenicol, or enrofloxacin based on culture/sensitivity. NSAIDs to reduce fever and inflammation. Severely ill animals may need IV fluids and vitamin supplementation.
Advice Prevention: Multi-pathogen respiratory vaccines (modified-live or killed) administered before periods of stress. Reduce co-mingling and transportation stress. Maintain clean, well-ventilated housing.

Treatment: IV fluid therapy (Lactated Ringer's + dextrose), hepatoprotective agents, antibiotics for secondary infections. Avoid corticosteroids which may worsen corneal edema ('blue eye'). Blood or plasma transfusion if needed.
Advice Prevention: DHPP (core) vaccination is highly protective. Annual or triennial boosters. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from potentially infected dogs and environments.

Treatment: IV fluid therapy is the cornerstone; antibiotics to prevent sepsis from gut bacteria translocation. Antiemetics and nutritional support (feeding tube if anorexic). Intensive care monitoring for temperature and hydration.
Advice Prevention: Core FVRCP vaccination from 6-8 weeks of age with boosters. The virus survives in the environment for months; disinfect with sodium hypochlorite.

Advice Prevention: Core CPV vaccination starting at 6-8 weeks; every 2-4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from dog parks and unknown dogs. Use parvocide disinfectants on contaminated areas.

Treatment: Supportive care: IV/SQ fluid therapy, nutritional support, and appetite stimulants. NSAIDs (meloxicam) for fever and oral ulcer pain; antibiotics (doxycycline) for secondary bacterial infections.
Advice Prevention: Annual FVRCP vaccination is the most effective preventive. Isolate infected cats immediately and disinfect shared surfaces with bleach solution. Reduce stress and overcrowding in multi-cat households.

Treatment: Hospitalization with IV crystalloid fluids, colloids if albumin is low, antiemetics (maropitant), and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Anti-endotoxin therapy; early enteral nutrition improves outcomes. Oseltamivir as adjunct in some protocols.

Treatment: Antibiotics (doxycycline or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for bacterial infections; antiviral famciclovir if herpesvirus involved. Nebulization, nasal decongestants, and nutritional support for recovery.
Advice Prevention: Vaccinate with FVRCP; isolate infected cats. Clean nasal discharge regularly; use a humidifier. Reduce stress and overcrowding. Wash hands between handling sick and healthy cats.

Treatment: • the external application of antiseptics contributes to the healing of the ulcers and wards off attacks by flies. • a common and inexpensive dressing for the lesions in the feet is a mixture of coal-tar and copper sulphate in the proportion of 5:1.
Advice Prevention: • heavy milch animals and exotic breeds of cattle bred for milk should be protected regularly. • it is advisable to carry out two vaccinations at an interval of six months followed by an annual vaccination programme. • isolation and segregation of sick animals. It should be informed immediately to the veterinary doctor • disinfection of animal sheds with bleaching powder or phenol • attendants and equipment’s for sick animals should be ideally separate • the equipment’s should be thoroughly sanitized • proper disposal of left over feed by the animal • proper disposal of carcasses • control of flies

Treatment: Treatment varies according to the condition. The following are some medicines that may be used; ; Intravenous or subcutaneous fluids in extreme cases of dehydration; Antibiotics in the presence of an infection; oral steroids such as prednisolone in the event of inflammation
Advice Prevention: Severe diarrhoea or vomiting may lead to dehydration. It is important to consult your veterinarian immediately.; offer your pet lots of fresh water to maintain hydration.; Change in diet is crucial to decrease digestive tract problems. Choose food which is high in soluble and insoluble fibre to be easily digestible.; Never feed your pet scrapes of skin or other left over’s which are not good for you. If they are not good for your neither are for your pet.

Treatment: Systemic antifungals based on fungus type: itraconazole or fluconazole for dermatophytes and yeast; terbinafine for ringworm; amphotericin B for systemic fungal infections. Topical antifungal shampoos and creams as adjuncts.
Advice Prevention: Keep skin dry and clean; treat all in-contact animals for ringworm simultaneously. Wear gloves during treatment. Disinfect environment with dilute bleach. Boost immune health through proper nutrition.

Treatment: Doxycycline (10 mg/kg orally once daily for 28-30 days) is the treatment of choice. Amoxicillin or azithromycin as alternatives. NSAIDs for joint pain relief. Repeat C6 antibody test at 6 months to assess response.
Advice Prevention: Lyme disease vaccination for dogs in endemic areas. Monthly tick preventives (isoxazolines: afoxolaner, fluralaner, sarolaner). Daily tick checks and prompt removal. Avoid wooded/tall-grass areas during peak tick season.

Treatment: Specific dewormers based on parasite: fenbendazole or pyrantel pamoate for roundworms/hookworms; praziquantel for tapeworms; metronidazole or fenbendazole for Giardia. Repeat treatment after 2-3 weeks.
Advice Prevention: Routine fecal examination twice yearly; monthly broad-spectrum parasite preventives. Clean litter box daily; prevent hunting of prey animals which carry tapeworm larvae.

Treatment: No specific antiviral treatment; intensive supportive care: IV fluids, anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, diazepam) for neurological signs, antibiotics for secondary infections, nutritional support. Vitamin A supplementation may be beneficial.
Advice Prevention: Core CDV vaccination is essential starting at 6-8 weeks; booster every 3-4 weeks until 16 weeks, then every 1-3 years. Keep unvaccinated puppies away from wildlife (raccoons, foxes are reservoirs).

Treatment: Intramammary antibiotic infusion (cloxacillin, penicillin-streptomycin, or cefapirin) directly into the affected teat after complete milk-out; systemic antibiotics (penicillin, amoxicillin) for severe cases with fever. NSAIDs (flunixin meglumine) for pain and inflammation.
Advice Prevention: Implement a dry-cow therapy program; dip teats in germicidal post-milking iodine solution after every milking. Maintain clean, dry bedding; cull chronically infected cows. Regular somatic cell count monitoring of bulk milk.
